headline: 40 Runs, One Perfect Sweep, and a Thriller That Had No Business Being That Close
subheadline: Week 2 of BeerLeagueBaseball delivered a league-record offensive explosion, a 9-0 demolition that should terrify the rest of the field, and one matchup so ugly it somehow ended 5-4.
body:
We're two weeks in and already the league has established its power brokers, its pretenders, and at least one team that needs to have a very serious conversation with their mirror. Week 2 was *not* subtle. We had a team score 40 runs. FORTY. (That's not a fantasy baseball number, that's a Little League mercy rule number.) We had a 9-0 sweep so complete it felt like a varsity team accidentally scheduled a JV squad. And we had a 5-4 thriller that somehow featured a 5.28 ERA on the winning side, which tells you everything you need to know about how chaotic this league remains.

Let's get into it.

**Stat of the Week: 40 — Sugar Land Skeeters scored a league-record 40 runs in a single week, obliterating the field en route to a dominant 8–1 demolition of Hiroshima Radiation.**

## At-a-Glance

| Winner | Loser | Score | Player of the Matchup |
|--------|-------|-------|-----------------------|
| North Correa | Albuquerque Bombers | 7-3 | North Correa's power surge (11 HR, 38 RBI, 2.85 ERA) — full roster dominance |
| Abbotsford-Colby Twins | Canberra Wombats | 6-4 | Abbotsford-Colby Twins' pitching staff (77 K, 2.25 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, 80 IP) |
| General Hospital | Athletic Park Dragons | 6-4 | General Hospital's pitching staff (2.10 ERA, 1.02 WHIP, league-record low ERA) |
| BaseballPro77 | Benchwarmers | 5-4 | Corbin Carroll (.519 OBP) — almost single-handedly stole it for the Benchwarmers |
| Riverside Roughnecks | Buttville Skidmarks | 7-3 | Riverside Roughnecks' offense (38 R, 10 HR, .365 OBP, 54 hits) |
| Sugar Land Skeeters | Hiroshima Radiation | 8-1 | Sugar Land Skeeters' entire roster (40 R, 67 K, 2.53 ERA, .346 OBP) |
| Wisconsin Rapids Reapers | Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs | 9-0 | Ben Rice (.593 OBP, 9 R, 3 HR) |

---

## Thriller of the Week

### BaseballPro77 def. Benchwarmers, 5-4

Nobody tell the **BaseballPro77** manager that posting a 5.28 ERA and a 1.43 WHIP is not typically a winning formula, because somehow they survived anyway. (It's giving "wins a bar fight but still needs stitches.") The **Benchwarmers** had Corbin Carroll going absolutely nuclear — a .519 OBP that is frankly illegal — and Cody Bellinger chipping in two steals to make things interesting, but it wasn't quite enough. **BaseballPro77** scraped together the pitching categories they needed and held on by a single category, which means their roster construction is either genius or a complete accident. (Probably the accident one.) Both teams sit in uncomfortable territory heading into Week 3, and one more loss for either could start a real conversation about their playoff viability. This one isn't over.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

### Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 9-0

Let me paint you a picture. The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** came into Week 2 with some genuine sleeper hype — the kind of team that gets named in the group chat as a "dark horse" contender. Then the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** showed up and did what can only be described as a complete and total erasure. Nine categories to zero. That doesn't happen. That's the fantasy baseball equivalent of the Globetrotters vs. the Washington Generals, except the Generals forgot to show up.

The Reapers were led by Ben Rice, who posted a jaw-dropping .593 OBP with 9 runs and 3 home runs — a performance so dominant it should come with a content warning. The pitching staff was somehow even more terrifying: a 0.84 WHIP that is not a real number and yet there it is, staring back at you in the spreadsheet. Seventy-five strikeouts. A 2.48 ERA. Eight saves/holds. The **Reapers** didn't just win every category — they won every category *convincingly*.

The **Wonderdogs**, meanwhile, managed 3 home runs, zero steals, and 16 runs scored for the entire week. (Zero steals! Not one! My 67-year-old father has more stolen bases than the Wonderdogs this week!) Back-to-back rough showings now have this team staring down a genuinely dangerous Week 3 matchup against the **Albuquerque Bombers**. One more loss and the "sleeper contender" narrative dies completely. The Reapers, on the other hand, just announced themselves as the team nobody wants to see in a playoff bracket.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### North Correa def. Albuquerque Bombers, 7-3

**North Correa** went full Shohei mode this week — 11 home runs, 38 RBI, 29 runs, and a 2.85 ERA from the pitching staff. The **Bombers** weren't terrible (63 strikeouts, 7 steals, a respectable NSVH of 3) but they brought a knife to a gunfight. North Correa dominated the power categories so thoroughly that the Bombers never had a chance to make it close. **Strategic implication:** North Correa has to be considered a top-three team in this league right now. **Next week:** The Bombers host the Wonderdogs — a winnable game that they need badly.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Canberra Wombats, 6-4

The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** did it with pitching and volume — 80 innings, 77 strikeouts, a 2.25 ERA, and 7 quality starts. The **Canberra Wombats** actually out-hit them (53 hits, 9 HR, 7 NSVH which was the best in the league this week) but couldn't overcome the pitching gap. The Twins' ability to eat innings is a real competitive advantage. **Strategic implication:** The Wombats' 7 saves/holds is bananas and their offense is fine — but they need their pitching to show up consistently. **Next week:** The Twins should be favored in whatever they draw.

### General Hospital def. Athletic Park Dragons, 6-4

**General Hospital** posted a 2.10 ERA and a 1.02 WHIP that would make any rotation manager weep with joy. Their .363 OBP led the offense. The **Athletic Park Dragons** had a brutal .267 OBP — which is "pitcher hitting in the NL" territory — and their 4.30 ERA wasn't helping anyone. **Strategic implication:** General Hospital is quietly one of the best-built rosters in the league. We'll get to why that matters in a second. **Next week:** Dragons need to find offense somewhere, fast.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Buttville Skidmarks, 7-3

The **Riverside Roughnecks** dropped 38 runs, 10 home runs, and a .365 OBP on the **Buttville Skidmarks**, and did it with a 2.42 ERA to boot. The Skidmarks' 3.19 ERA was actually solid — but when you're only scoring 26 runs against a team scoring 38, the pitching doesn't matter. **Strategic implication:** The Roughnecks are for real. This wasn't a fluke week — the balance of offense and pitching is genuinely scary. **Next week:** The Skidmarks need to find some run production or they're going to keep dropping close-ish matchups.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Hiroshima Radiation, 8-1

FORTY RUNS. I need to say that again. **Sugar Land Skeeters** scored *forty runs* in a single week. That's a Tecmo Bowl number. That's a "did the opponent's pitching staff actually pitch or just send their Wi-Fi password?" number. The **Hiroshima Radiation** managed 27 runs and a competent 3.29 ERA, but the Skeeters also posted 67 strikeouts and a 2.53 ERA — so this was a comprehensive beatdown, not just an offensive outlier. **Strategic implication:** The Skeeters just reset the league's expectations for what a ceiling performance looks like. **Next week:** Anyone facing the Skeeters should probably start stress-eating now.

### Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 9-0

*(Already covered above — but seriously, 0.84 WHIP. I cannot stop thinking about it.)*

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers**

Look, I don't want to be mean, but the **Benchwarmers** have to know they caught a break here. A 4.89 ERA, a 1.50 WHIP, and only 42 strikeouts — and they still nearly won? Their luck index of +0.58 is the highest in the league, which is a very polite way of saying: their record is lying about how good they are. They're the team that keeps finding $20 bills on the sidewalk despite not leaving the house. Corbin Carroll is doing the Lord's work for this roster, but the pitching needs to stop being a disaster before their luck runs out completely. (And it will run out. It always runs out.)

**Unlucky Team: General Hospital**

The **General Hospital** manager deserves a hug and a strong drink. A 2.10 ERA. A 1.02 WHIP. A .363 OBP. A 6-4 victory that somehow generated a luck delta of -0.52 — tied for the worst in the league. They are doing everything RIGHT and the universe is shrugging at them. This is the "gets straight A's but never gets invited to the party" team. Their numbers suggest they should be a top-two or three team in the league by record. They're not. If the fantasy gods have any sense of justice, the General Hospital manager gets rewarded eventually. But this is fantasy baseball, so probably not.

---

## League Records Broken

Let's give these their proper respect:

- 🏆 **North Correa** — New season record for most HR in a week with **11** (tied/confirmed their own previous mark) and most RBI with **38**. The power ceiling in this league just got officially redefined.
- 🏆 **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — New season record for most strikeouts by a pitching staff with **77 K**, surpassing the previous high of 75 set by the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** *this same week*. Two teams broke the record in the same week. In the same week! That's the most fantasy baseball thing that's ever happened.
- 🏆 **General Hospital** — New season record for lowest ERA by a winning team at **2.10**. They set a record and still only won 6-4. See "Unlucky Team" above.

---

## Manager Spotlight: BaseballPro77

The **BaseballPro77** manager is either a mad genius or extremely lucky, and honestly after Week 2, I'm not sure it matters which. They went 5-4 with a 5.28 ERA — a number that would make most managers curl up in a ball and stare at the waiver wire for six hours. Their offense is functional but unspectacular (19 runs, a .277 OBP that is not going to win many weeks), and the pitching has been their Achilles heel through two weeks.

Here's the thing though: they're finding ways to win. Five categories is five categories. They're 2-0, sitting comfortably in the top half of the standings, and they haven't even needed their roster to perform well to get there. If the pitching stabilizes — and with 60 strikeouts and 5 NSVH, there's something to work with — this team could be genuinely dangerous by midseason. The playoff outlook is cautiously optimistic, the kind of "yeah, they'll probably be there" that makes you nervous to play them in October. The real question is whether they can survive a matchup against one of the genuinely elite pitching staffs in this league (looking at you, **Reapers** and **Twins**) before their ERA becomes an emergency.

---

## Trade Value: Buy, Sell, or Hold?

**Ben Rice (Wisconsin Rapids Reapers)** — Posted a .593 OBP, 9 runs, and 3 home runs in a single week, anchoring a 9-0 sweep. **VERDICT: BUY.** Two weeks of evidence suggest he's a building block, not a fluke. If he's available in your league, you're already too late.

**Corbin Carroll (Benchwarmers)** — A .519 OBP that nearly carried his team to an upset win despite the pitching being a crime scene. **VERDICT: HOLD.** Carroll's on-base ability is elite and he adds steals — but the Benchwarmers' overall construction means you might be able to pry him loose at a slight discount if you frame it as "helping both teams." Worth monitoring.

**Any Hiroshima Radiation pitcher** — They allowed 40 runs. Forty. Their staff posted a 3.29 ERA which is fine, but the run support they surrendered is a red flag about their underlying setup. **VERDICT: SELL** if you're holding them for saves/holds and your playoff window is now. The **Skeeters** made them look mortal in a way that's hard to unsee.

---

## Power Rankings

*(Week 1 — no movement arrows yet. Everyone's starting somewhere.)*

1. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — 9-0 sweep. 0.84 WHIP. Enough said.
2. **Sugar Land Skeeters** — Forty. Runs. League record. Call your shot.
3. **Riverside Roughnecks** — Balanced, efficient, and scoring at will. Quietly terrifying.
4. **North Correa** — Power numbers that break records. Legit title contender.
5. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — The pitching depth is a genuine weapon.
6. **General Hospital** — Best stats, middling record. The universe owes them.
7. **BaseballPro77** — 2-0 and we still don't know what they are. Interesting!
8. **Canberra Wombats** — 7 saves/holds, offense that can hang. Middling is okay for now.
9. **Albuquerque Bombers** — Lost but weren't embarrassed. Need a bounce-back.
10. **Buttville Skidmarks** — Good pitching, not enough offense. Something has to give.
11. **Hiroshima Radiation** — Getting lit up for 40 runs will do things to your ranking.
12. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — 0 steals, 9-0 loss. The sleeper hype is officially on pause. Wake me up when something changes.

---

## Waiver Wire

No notable moves this week — which, honestly, given how chaotic the results were, is surprising. If your team scored 40 runs, you probably don't need to touch anything. If your team got swept 9-0, you should probably be burning the waiver wire to the ground right now. The **Wonderdogs** in particular need to be aggressive — zero steals through Week 2 is a category you're just handing to your opponents, and that's a correctable problem if you move fast.

Check your leagues. Make your adds. Week
